title: What an American thinks about Bosnia-Herzegovina bike touring 2010

transcript.text: Okay, this video is what an American thinks about Bosnia-Herzegovina. I was in Sarajevo for three days, three nights, two full days, you know, and then I rode out of it the next day.
Bosnia-Herzegovina is a very pretty city, pretty country. The whole time I was there it was very pretty. I was enjoying myself, you know.

I started across the border and then I went, oh, I remember, yeah, and then it went, yeah, I mean, the whole country, like, I went up a river bed, kind of looked like the Rhine, and then I went up hills, really up, up, up, up, up, up, up, up, over this, like, 4,200-bit pass, and then I went down again, you know, for, like, a few hours, and they had, like, it was the first time I saw those huge, like, cone-shaped hay bales, and they have them, you know, all over since then, but there's big, big, big littering, though.
I couldn't believe it. I mean, like, pristine land, beautiful, beautiful land, but bottles, plastic bottles everywhere, you know, floating down the river.
Same thing in Serbia, but, yeah, also in Bosnia-Herzegovina, and then I went over another pass, it was, like, 4,200 feet, and then it, like, went straight the fuck down into Sarajevo.
Like, at 7, I looked at my watch, and I was in, you know, at the top of this pass, and I was like, oh, that's pretty, you know, and I had no idea how far it was to Sarajevo.

I thought it was pretty far, but half hour later, I was in Sarajevo, because it was all downhill, straight downhill, all the way to Sarajevo, and it's beautiful, and it's, like, the old part of town in Sarajevo,
Which is pretty much, like, the only, really, place interesting to look at, to see, unless you see, like, that tunnel museum, which I didn't actually go to.

But, like, right when you, like, literally right, right when you're in the city, you go through, like, a tunnel, and you go through, like, into the, into the, like, the valley they're in, like, around this, like, cliff-looking thing, and boom, that's the center of the city.
So, going into Sarajevo is kind of like going into a tiny little town, basically. That's basically what the experience is.
And then the pedestrian malls are amazing. They're, like, really old, and you can feel the ancientness of them, you know, like the ghosts of all the time past.
And there's, like, there's not just one, but you have, like, two or, like, three, or at least two parallel to each other, and then, you know, little ones that connect them.
And then one that's really long, so, and they're, they're narrow, and it's more, like, ancient and probably, you know, more pleasant than even Prague.
Prague is really wide, you know, and modern, but this is, like, ancient, you know.
It's totally touristy. Great place, but I didn't see a lot of tourists there.
But, yeah, the Bosnians are really nice, you know, waving to me and stuff, honking at me.
But, yeah, there were, like, bullet holes all over the buildings, which is interesting that I saw looking at them.
I went to, you know, the place, outskirts where those big apartment buildings are.
And I saw some few cops looking for that tunnel.
There's a tunnel where they were under siege for four years in, like, 92 to 96 or something like that, with the Serbians shelling them the whole time.
And they only had one way to get stuff in and out, and that was through a tunnel that's, like, I guess you have to crawl on your head or knees.
And the electric, the electricity was supplied through the tunnel, too, so if you stand up, you get fried.
And they had to push these little carts through this tunnel in and out, you know, from the airport into the city that was, I can't remember how long it was, but a couple miles or some.
They were pretty trippy.
And, um, they speak pretty good English.
I didn't really hang out with that many people, but, yeah, they spoke pretty good English there.
I guess the same as the Serbians.
They kind of like the same as the Serbians is.
And then south of, um, of, um, this place, um, Sarajevo was really beautiful.
Like, it went right up those valleys and then up a, up a hill, you know, I saw the ski resorts on the mountains.
Like, you know, like, looked like Colorado.
Like, it still had snow on them and they had, they were above timberline.
And then it went down.
And then I went, like, on this tiny little road on the side of a hill.
On the side of, like, a mountain, you know, overlooking this, like, really colorful, like, turquoise river.
And, uh, for a long time, tiny road.
Like a bike pass side of the road.
And I was like, am I on the right road to get to the next country?
And, yeah.
Then I crossed the river where the border crossing was and went into Montenegro.
So, sorry, Bosnia-Herzegovina is a very beautiful place.
Um, and mountainy, um, you know, more than Serbia.
All of what I saw with Serbia was flat.
You get to Bosnia and that's where the hills start.
And, uh, Croatia, too.
You know, Croatia, a lot of Croatia was flat, but it was hilly, a lot hillier than Serbia.
So, uh, that's what an American thinks about Bosnia-Herzegovina.
